title: Fuel-adjusted latent tyre-state filter with reset and cliff features
summary: Recent F1 degradation papers model tyre pace as a latent state with stint resets after pit stops; they also note roughly linear mid-stint decay but structural resets and observation noise from traffic/errors.
rationale: Recent F1 degradation papers model tyre pace as a latent state with stint resets after pit stops; they also note roughly linear mid-stint decay but structural resets and observation noise from traffic/errors. That suggests a compact online filter may produce cleaner signals than raw `LapTime_Delta` and cumulative degradation alone.
implementation_hint: For each driver-race stint, estimate latent clean-air pace and degradation slope with a simple Kalman/state-space approximation using lap time, tyre life, and race progress; reset state after `PitStop=1`. Feed the model filtered level, slope, acceleration, innovation residual, and a ‘cliff probability’ flag when residuals deteriorate faster than the fitted slope.
expected_effect: May sharpen the separation between normal old-tyre operation and genuine pre-stop collapse, particularly when traffic makes raw lap times noisy.
risk: If the filter assumptions are wrong, it can just repackage existing features and add noise; fuel and traffic are only indirectly observed in this dataset.
